refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up / total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999. Requires instrumentation of link health across all network segments.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: Detect teleoperation link failures within acceptable latency (e.g., <500 ms)
FR-NGHE-010.2: Maintain command uplink integrity and latency within operational bounds
FR-NGHE-010.3: Maintain telemetry downlink integrity and latency within operational bounds
FR-NGHE-010.4: Recover from transient link failures and restore availability
FR-NGHE-010.5: Monitor and report link availability metrics to stakeholders

candidate_dps: DP-A: Redundant network paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; dual radio/cellular modems with independent carriers; continuous health monitoring and sub-second switchover
DP-B: Single high-reliability network path with error correction and retransmission; robust protocol stack with ARQ; link quality monitoring and graceful degradation
DP-C: Hybrid approach: primary path with redundant backup; dual-modem architecture with carrier diversity; active health monitoring; automatic failover + link quality adaptation
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid redundancy with dual-modem architecture, carrier diversity, active health monitoring, and automatic failover to achieve 0.999 availability. Primary path carries normal traffic; backup path remains hot-standby. Health checks every 100 ms; failover latency <500 ms. Link quality metrics inform adaptive coding/modulation.

potential_sources: DP-C couples to network infrastructure (carrier availability, signal coverage) — external dependency, not a design coupling
DP-C couples to Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface) — required for link-loss safety response; acceptable coupling, well-defined interface
DP-C couples to Teleoperation module (command/telemetry processing) — expected; interface is Tasking (in) and Telemetry (out)
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is decoupled from vehicle propulsion/actuation logic. Link availability is orthogonal to what commands are sent. Coupling to Safety Services is intentional and safety-critical: link loss must trigger safe state. No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dual-modem hardware is available and cost-acceptable for NGHE platform
Assumption: Two independent cellular/radio carriers with sufficient coverage at worksite
Risk: Carrier outages or degradation could reduce availability below 0.999 if both carriers fail simultaneously
Risk: Failover latency <500 ms may not be achievable in all network conditions; requires validation
Risk: Dual-modem power consumption and thermal management must be addressed in vehicle design
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) modem selection criteria (e.g., LTE Cat-6 + 5G NR dual-stack), (2) carrier selection strategy (geographic coverage overlap, SLA requirements), (3) health-check protocol (ping interval, timeout threshold), (4) failover decision logic (e.g., link quality metric threshold), (5) power budget allocation for dual modems. Add contingency: if dual-carrier availability is <0.999, consider satellite backup or mesh relay.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-modem architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and telemetry/tasking protocol handling. Includes modem drivers, link state machine, health-check scheduler, and failover controller. Interfaces with Vehicle for power/thermal management and with Safety Services for link-loss alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (e.g., move, stop, emergency) from tele-operator via Teleoperation module; must be delivered with <latency_bound> and integrity guarantee

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link quality metrics) to tele-operator; must be delivered with <latency_bound> and sufficient frequency for safe remote control

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals link-loss or link-degradation events to Safety Services; triggers safe-state actions (e.g., vehicle halt, alarm) when link availability drops below threshold

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstraction of dual-modem hardware and carrier connectivity; Teleoperation module manages link selection, health monitoring, and failover; Network Link provides status (up/down, signal strength, latency) and accepts commands (switch modem, adjust coding)

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Dual-modem management, failover, and health monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert coupling (intentional, safety-critical). High containment.

Vehicle owns propulsion and actuation; link management is not a vehicle concern. If assigned here, link logic would be scattered across vehicle subsystems, creating diffusion and maintenance burden. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policies and response logic (e.g., safe-state actions on link loss). Link availability monitoring is not a safety service concern; it is a communication infrastructure concern. Assigning here would blur safety vs. communication responsibilities.
NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning link availability here would leave implementation scattered across sub-modules. Not a suitable boundary for detailed DP realization.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns all dual-modem hardware drivers, link state machine, health-check logic, and failover controller. It exposes link status via Network Link interface and signals link-loss events via SafetyAlert to Safety Services. This centralizes link availability logic and minimizes diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module also manages network hardware (e.g., modem power, antenna placement), coordination is required to avoid diffusion. Recommend clear ownership: Teleoperation owns link logic; Vehicle owns physical modem placement and power supply.

Risk: If Safety Services needs to query link status for safety decisions, SafetyAlert interface may become bidirectional (inout). Clarify: does Safety Services need real-time link status, or only link-loss events?

Risk: Network Link interface abstraction must be well-defined to avoid tight coupling between Teleoperation and modem hardware. Recommend hardware abstraction layer (HAL) to decouple modem drivers from link logic.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation module boundary to explicitly include: (1) Dual-modem driver layer (HAL), (2) Link state machine (up/down/degraded states), (3) Health-check scheduler and timeout logic, (4) Failover decision engine, (5) Link quality metric aggregator. Clarify interfaces: Tasking (in, command delivery), Telemetry (out, state + link metrics), SafetyAlert (out, link-loss events), Network Link (inout, modem control + status). Add internal sub-modules: LinkHealthMonitor, FailoverController, ModemDriver.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid redundancy with dual-modem failover) directly addresses FR requirement for 0.999 availability. Dual-modem architecture with carrier diversity and sub-500ms failover is a proven approach for achieving high availability in remote control systems. DP-C is implementable, testable, and aligns with safety-critical constraints. No fundamental mismatch between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If modem drivers are scattered across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules, link logic becomes fragmented. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns all link-layer logic; Vehicle provides only physical modem placement and power.

Diffusion risk: If Safety Services also monitors link status independently, redundant monitoring logic emerges. Mitigation: Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation; no independent link monitoring.
Diffusion risk: If Network Link interface is not well-abstracted, Teleoperation becomes tightly coupled to specific modem hardware. Mitigation: Define HAL to decouple link logic from modem drivers.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score for link availability logic. Dual-modem management, failover, and health monitoring are cohesive and belong in Teleoperation. SafetyAlert coupling to Safety Services is intentional and safety-critical; acceptable. No significant diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) Instrument Teleoperation module to log link state transitions and failover events. (2) Measure link availability as (time_link_up / total_time) during evaluation period. (3) Inject link faults (modem failure, carrier outage) to validate failover latency and recovery. (4) Validate SafetyAlert triggering on link loss. Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns link availability DP. Sub-FRs (link failure detection, uplink/downlink integrity, recovery, metrics reporting) map to Teleoperation sub-modules (LinkHealthMonitor, FailoverController, ModemDriver, MetricsAggregator). Each sub-FR has a clear owner and verification method.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify modem selection criteria, carrier strategy, health-check protocol, and failover decision logic.
Clarify Teleoperation module boundary to include HAL, link state machine, health-check scheduler, and failover controller.
Define Network Link interface abstraction to decouple link logic from modem hardware.
Specify SafetyAlert interface: is it one-way (link-loss events only) or bidirectional (link status queries)?
Add contingency plan: if dual-carrier availability is <0.999, consider satellite backup or mesh relay.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into five sub-FRs (link failure detection, uplink/downlink integrity, recovery, metrics reporting). DP-C is sound and well-aligned with FR. Teleoperation module is the correct owner with 0.95 containment. However, to ensure complete coverage and avoid gaps, we must decompose each sub-FR and assign DPs and IMs at the next level. Specifically, sub-FRs 010.1 (failure detection), 010.2 (uplink integrity), 010.3 (downlink integrity), 010.4 (recovery), and 010.5 (metrics) require detailed DPs and module assignments.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: Detect teleoperation link failures within <500 ms latency
FR-NGHE-010.2: Maintain command uplink integrity and latency <latency_bound (e.g., 100 ms)
FR-NGHE-010.3: Maintain telemetry downlink integrity and latency <latency_bound (e.g., 100 ms)
FR-NGHE-010.4: Recover from transient link failures and restore availability within <recovery_time (e.g., 1 s)
FR-NGHE-010.5: Monitor and report link availability metrics (uptime %, failover count, latency distribution) to stakeholders

assumptions: Dual-modem hardware (LTE + 5G or dual-LTE) is available and cost-acceptable for NGHE platform.
Two independent cellular carriers with sufficient coverage at typical worksites are available.
Failover latency <500 ms is achievable with modern modem hardware and link state machine logic.
Safety Services will consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation and trigger safe-state actions (e.g., vehicle halt) on link loss.
Evaluation period for availability measurement is ≥30 days to ensure statistical significance.
Tele-operator and Site Manager accept 0.999 availability target; no higher target (e.g., 0.9999) is required.
open_risks: Carrier outage or degradation could reduce availability below 0.999 if both carriers fail simultaneously. Mitigation: select carriers with geographic diversity and high SLA; consider satellite backup.

Failover latency <500 ms may not be achievable in all network conditions (e.g., poor signal, high congestion). Mitigation: validate failover latency under worst-case conditions; adjust target if needed.
Dual-modem power consumption and thermal management could impact vehicle battery life and operating temperature. Mitigation: conduct power budget analysis; optimize modem duty cycle.

SafetyAlert interface coupling to Safety Services could create circular dependencies if Safety Services also monitors link status. Mitigation: clarify ownership; Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert only.
Network Link interface abstraction may leak modem-specific details if not carefully designed. Mitigation: define HAL to decouple link logic from hardware.
evidence_needed: Modem hardware selection and carrier SLA documentation.
Link state machine design and failover decision logic specification.
Health-check protocol specification (ping interval, timeout threshold, link quality metrics).
Failover latency measurement under various network conditions (good signal, poor signal, carrier outage).
Power budget analysis for dual-modem architecture.
SafetyAlert interface specification and Safety Services integration plan.
Network Link HAL design and modem driver implementation.
Availability measurement methodology and evaluation period definition.
Test plan for link failure injection and failover validation.
Contingency plan for satellite backup or mesh relay if dual-carrier availability is insufficient.
